assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(k1,_), &&(k2, _)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;(1, 2)</code>);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, 2), (2, 2), (3, 9), (4, 8), (5, 9)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(_,k1), &&(_,k2)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;(3, 9), &amp;(5, 9)</code>);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(k1,_), &&(k2, _)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;(1, 2), &amp;(1, 3), &amp;(1, 4), &amp;(1, 5)</code>);

    The elements can be floats but no particular result is guaranteed if an element is NaN.

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [(i32, i32); 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|_, _| Ordering::Equal), Vec::<&(i32, i32)>::new());

let a = [(1, 2)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(k1,_), &&(k2, _)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec![&(1, 2)]);

let a = [(1, 2), (2, 2), (3, 9), (4, 8), (5, 9)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(_,k1), &&(_,k2)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec![&(3, 9), &(5, 9)]);

let a = [(1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(k1,_), &&(k2, _)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec![&(1, 2), &(1, 3), &(1, 4), &(1, 5)]);

    use std::cmp::Ordering;
use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [(i32, i32); 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|_, _| Ordering::Equal), Vec::<&(i32, i32)>::new());

let a = [(1, 2)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(k1,_), &&(k2, _)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec![&(1, 2)]);

let a = [(1, 2), (2, 2), (3, 9), (4, 8), (5, 9)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(_,k1), &&(_,k2)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec![&(3, 9), &(5, 9)]);

let a = [(1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by(|&&(k1,_), &&(k2, _)| k1.cmp(&k2)), vec![&(1, 2), &(1, 3), &(1, 4), &(1, 5)]);
    Run
    Source

    max_set_by_key

    fn max_set_by_key(self: Self, key: F) -> Vec<<Self as >::Item>

    Return all maximum elements of an iterator, as determined by the specified function.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(i32, i32); 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|_| ()), Vec::<&(i32, i32)>::new());

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, 2)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|&&(k,_)| k), vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;(1, 2)</code>);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, 2), (2, 2), (3, 9), (4, 8), (5, 9)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|&&(_, k)| k), vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;(3, 9), &amp;(5, 9)</code>);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|&&(k, _)| k), vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;(1, 2), &amp;(1, 3), &amp;(1, 4), &amp;(1, 5)</code>);

    The elements can be floats but no particular result is guaranteed if an element is NaN.

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [(i32, i32); 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|_| ()), Vec::<&(i32, i32)>::new());

let a = [(1, 2)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|&&(k,_)| k), vec![&(1, 2)]);

let a = [(1, 2), (2, 2), (3, 9), (4, 8), (5, 9)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|&&(_, k)| k), vec![&(3, 9), &(5, 9)]);

let a = [(1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5)];
assert_eq!(a.iter().max_set_by_key(|&&(k, _)| k), vec![&(1, 2), &(1, 3), &(1, 4), &(1, 5)]);
    Run
    Source

    minmax

    fn minmax(self: Self) -> MinMaxResult<<Self as >::Item>

    Return the minimum and maximum elements in the iterator.

    The return type MinMaxResult is an enum of three variants:

    • NoElements if the iterator is empty.
    • OneElement(x) if the iterator has exactly one element.
    • MinMax(x, y) is returned otherwise, where x <= y. Two values are equal if and only if there is more than one element in the iterator and all elements are equal.

    On an iterator of length n, minmax does 1.5 * n comparisons, and so is faster than calling min and max separately which does 2 * n comparisons.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), NoElements);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), OneElement(&1));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 2, 3, 4, 5</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&1, &5));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 1, 1, 1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&1, &1));
    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(i32, char); 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), NoElements);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, &#39;a&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), OneElement(&(1, 'a')));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(0, &#39;a&#39;), (1, &#39;b&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&(0, 'a'), &(1, 'b')));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, &#39;a&#39;), (1, &#39;b&#39;), (1, &#39;c&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&(1, 'a'), &(1, 'c')));

    The elements can be floats but no particular result is guaranteed if an element is NaN.

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), NoElements);

let a = [1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), OneElement(&1));

let a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&1, &5));

let a = [1, 1, 1, 1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&1, &1));
    Run
    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: [(i32, char); 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), NoElements);

let a = [(1, 'a')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), OneElement(&(1, 'a')));

let a = [(0, 'a'), (1, 'b')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&(0, 'a'), &(1, 'b')));

let a = [(1, 'a'), (1, 'b'), (1, 'c')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax(), MinMax(&(1, 'a'), &(1, 'c')));
    Run
    Source

    minmax_by_key

    fn minmax_by_key(self: Self, key: F) -> MinMaxResult<<Self as >::Item>

    Return the minimum and maximum element of an iterator, as determined by the specified function.

    The return value is a variant of MinMaxResult like for .minmax().

    For the minimum, the first minimal element is returned. For the maximum, the last maximal element wins. This matches the behavior of the standard Iterator::min and Iterator::max methods.

    The keys can be floats but no particular result is guaranteed if a key is NaN.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let cmp_key = |x: &&(i32, char)| x.0;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(i32, char); 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), NoElements);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, &#39;a&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), OneElement(&(1, 'a')));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(0, &#39;a&#39;), (1, &#39;b&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), MinMax(&(0, 'a'), &(1, 'b')));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, &#39;a&#39;), (1, &#39;b&#39;), (1, &#39;c&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), MinMax(&(1, 'a'), &(1, 'c')));
    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let cmp_key = |x: &&(i32, char)| x.0;

let a: [(i32, char); 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), NoElements);

let a = [(1, 'a')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), OneElement(&(1, 'a')));

let a = [(0, 'a'), (1, 'b')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), MinMax(&(0, 'a'), &(1, 'b')));

let a = [(1, 'a'), (1, 'b'), (1, 'c')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by_key(cmp_key), MinMax(&(1, 'a'), &(1, 'c')));
    Run
    Source

    minmax_by

    fn minmax_by(self: Self, compare: F) -> MinMaxResult<<Self as >::Item>

    Return the minimum and maximum element of an iterator, as determined by the specified comparison function.

    The return value is a variant of MinMaxResult like for .minmax().

    For the minimum, the first minimal element is returned. For the maximum, the last maximal element wins. This matches the behavior of the standard Iterator::min and Iterator::max methods.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let first_item_cmp = |x: &&(i32, char), y: &&(i32, char)| x.0.cmp(&y.0);

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(i32, char); 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), NoElements);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, &#39;a&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), OneElement(&(1, 'a')));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(0, &#39;a&#39;), (1, &#39;b&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), MinMax(&(0, 'a'), &(1, 'b')));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, &#39;a&#39;), (1, &#39;b&#39;), (1, &#39;c&#39;)</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), MinMax(&(1, 'a'), &(1, 'c')));
    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let first_item_cmp = |x: &&(i32, char), y: &&(i32, char)| x.0.cmp(&y.0);

let a: [(i32, char); 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), NoElements);

let a = [(1, 'a')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), OneElement(&(1, 'a')));

let a = [(0, 'a'), (1, 'b')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), MinMax(&(0, 'a'), &(1, 'b')));

let a = [(1, 'a'), (1, 'b'), (1, 'c')];
assert_eq!(a.iter().minmax_by(first_item_cmp), MinMax(&(1, 'a'), &(1, 'c')));
    Run
    Source

    position_max

    fn position_max(self: Self) -> Option<usize>

    Return the position of the maximum element in the iterator.

    If several elements are equally maximum, the position of the last of them is returned.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max(), None);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max(), Some(3));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max(), Some(1));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max(), None);

let a = [-3, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max(), Some(3));

let a = [1, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max(), Some(1));
    Run
    Source

    position_max_by_key

    fn position_max_by_key(self: Self, key: F) -> Option<usize>

    Return the position of the maximum element in the iterator, as determined by the specified function.

    If several elements are equally maximum, the position of the last of them is returned.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by_key(|x| x.abs()), None);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(4));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1_i32, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(3));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by_key(|x| x.abs()), None);

let a = [-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(4));

let a = [1_i32, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(3));
    Run
    Source

    position_max_by

    fn position_max_by(self: Self, compare: F) -> Option<usize>

    Return the position of the maximum element in the iterator, as determined by the specified comparison function.

    If several elements are equally maximum, the position of the last of them is returned.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), None);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(3));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1_i32, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(1));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), None);

let a = [-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(3));

let a = [1_i32, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_max_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(1));
    Run
    Source

    position_min

    fn position_min(self: Self) -> Option<usize>

    Return the position of the minimum element in the iterator.

    If several elements are equally minimum, the position of the first of them is returned.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min(), None);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min(), Some(4));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min(), Some(2));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min(), None);

let a = [-3, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min(), Some(4));

let a = [1, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min(), Some(2));
    Run
    Source

    position_min_by_key

    fn position_min_by_key(self: Self, key: F) -> Option<usize>

    Return the position of the minimum element in the iterator, as determined by the specified function.

    If several elements are equally minimum, the position of the first of them is returned.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by_key(|x| x.abs()), None);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(1));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1_i32, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(0));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by_key(|x| x.abs()), None);

let a = [-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(1));

let a = [1_i32, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by_key(|x| x.abs()), Some(0));
    Run
    Source

    position_min_by

    fn position_min_by(self: Self, compare: F) -> Option<usize>

    Return the position of the minimum element in the iterator, as determined by the specified comparison function.

    If several elements are equally minimum, the position of the first of them is returned.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), None);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(4));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1_i32, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(2));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), None);

let a = [-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(4));

let a = [1_i32, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_min_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), Some(2));
    Run
    Source

    position_minmax

    fn position_minmax(self: Self) -> MinMaxResult<usize>

    Return the positions of the minimum and maximum elements in the iterator.

    The return type MinMaxResult is an enum of three variants:

    • NoElements if the iterator is empty.
    • OneElement(xpos) if the iterator has exactly one element.
    • MinMax(xpos, ypos) is returned otherwise, where the element at xpos ≤ the element at ypos. While the referenced elements themselves may be equal, xpos cannot be equal to ypos.

    On an iterator of length n, position_minmax does 1.5 * n comparisons, and so is faster than calling position_min and position_max separately which does 2 * n comparisons.

    For the minimum, if several elements are equally minimum, the position of the first of them is returned. For the maximum, if several elements are equally maximum, the position of the last of them is returned.

    The elements can be floats but no particular result is guaranteed if an element is NaN.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), NoElements);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), OneElement(0));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), MinMax(4, 3));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), MinMax(2, 1));
    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), NoElements);

let a = [10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), OneElement(0));

let a = [-3, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), MinMax(4, 3));

let a = [1, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax(), MinMax(2, 1));
    Run
    Source

    position_minmax_by_key

    fn position_minmax_by_key(self: Self, key: F) -> MinMaxResult<usize>

    Return the positions of the minimum and maximum elements of an iterator, as determined by the specified function.

    The return value is a variant of MinMaxResult like for position_minmax.

    For the minimum, if several elements are equally minimum, the position of the first of them is returned. For the maximum, if several elements are equally maximum, the position of the last of them is returned.

    The keys can be floats but no particular result is guaranteed if a key is NaN.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), NoElements);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">10_i32</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), OneElement(0));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), MinMax(1, 4));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1_i32, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), MinMax(0, 3));

    position_minmax: Self::position_minmax

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), NoElements);

let a = [10_i32];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), OneElement(0));

let a = [-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), MinMax(1, 4));

let a = [1_i32, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by_key(|x| x.abs()), MinMax(0, 3));
    Run
    Source

    position_minmax_by

    fn position_minmax_by(self: Self, compare: F) -> MinMaxResult<usize>

    Return the positions of the minimum and maximum elements of an iterator, as determined by the specified comparison function.

    The return value is a variant of MinMaxResult like for position_minmax.

    For the minimum, if several elements are equally minimum, the position of the first of them is returned. For the maximum, if several elements are equally maximum, the position of the last of them is returned.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">i32; 0</code> = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), NoElements);

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">10_i32</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), OneElement(0));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), MinMax(4, 3));

let a = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1_i32, 1, -1, -1</code>;
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), MinMax(2, 1));

    position_minmax: Self::position_minmax

    use itertools::Itertools;
use itertools::MinMaxResult::{MinMax, NoElements, OneElement};

let a: [i32; 0] = [];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), NoElements);

let a = [10_i32];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), OneElement(0));

let a = [-3_i32, 0, 1, 5, -10];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), MinMax(4, 3));

let a = [1_i32, 1, -1, -1];
assert_eq!(a.iter().position_minmax_by(|x, y| x.cmp(y)), MinMax(2, 1));
    Run
    Source

    exactly_one

    fn exactly_one(self: Self) -> Result<<Self as >::Item, ExactlyOneError<Self>>

    If the iterator yields exactly one element, that element will be returned, otherwise an error will be returned containing an iterator that has the same output as the input iterator.

    This provides an additional layer of validation over just calling Iterator::next(). If your assumption that there should only be one element yielded is false this provides the opportunity to detect and handle that, preventing errors at a distance.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

assert_eq!((0..10).filter(|&x| x == 2).exactly_one().unwrap(), 2);
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 4).exactly_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..4));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 5).exactly_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..5));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&_| false).exactly_one().unwrap_err().eq(0..0));
    use itertools::Itertools;

assert_eq!((0..10).filter(|&x| x == 2).exactly_one().unwrap(), 2);
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 4).exactly_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..4));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 5).exactly_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..5));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&_| false).exactly_one().unwrap_err().eq(0..0));
    Run
    Source

    at_most_one

    fn at_most_one(self: Self) -> Result<Option<<Self as >::Item>, ExactlyOneError<Self>>

    If the iterator yields no elements, Ok(None) will be returned. If the iterator yields exactly one element, that element will be returned, otherwise an error will be returned containing an iterator that has the same output as the input iterator.

    This provides an additional layer of validation over just calling Iterator::next(). If your assumption that there should be at most one element yielded is false this provides the opportunity to detect and handle that, preventing errors at a distance.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

assert_eq!((0..10).filter(|&x| x == 2).at_most_one().unwrap(), Some(2));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 4).at_most_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..4));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 5).at_most_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..5));
assert_eq!((0..10).filter(|&_| false).at_most_one().unwrap(), None);
    use itertools::Itertools;

assert_eq!((0..10).filter(|&x| x == 2).at_most_one().unwrap(), Some(2));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 4).at_most_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..4));
assert!((0..10).filter(|&x| x > 1 && x < 5).at_most_one().unwrap_err().eq(2..5));
assert_eq!((0..10).filter(|&_| false).at_most_one().unwrap(), None);
    Run
    Source

    multipeek

    fn multipeek(self: Self) -> MultiPeek<Self>

    An iterator adaptor that allows the user to peek at multiple .next() values without advancing the base iterator.

    Examples

    use itertools::Itertools;

let mut iter = (0..10).multipeek();
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&0));
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&1));
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&2));
assert_eq!(iter.next(), Some(0));
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&1));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let mut iter = (0..10).multipeek();
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&0));
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&1));
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&2));
assert_eq!(iter.next(), Some(0));
assert_eq!(iter.peek(), Some(&1));
    Run
    Source

    counts

    fn counts(self: Self) -> HashMap<<Self as >::Item, usize>

    Collect the items in this iterator and return a HashMap which contains each item that appears in the iterator and the number of times it appears.

    Examples

    # use itertools::Itertools;
let counts = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 1, 1, 3, 3, 5</code>.iter().counts();
assert_eq!(counts<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;1</code>, 3);
assert_eq!(counts<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;3</code>, 2);
assert_eq!(counts<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;5</code>, 1);
assert_eq!(counts.get(&0), None);
    let counts = [1, 1, 1, 3, 3, 5].iter().counts();
assert_eq!(counts[&1], 3);
assert_eq!(counts[&3], 2);
assert_eq!(counts[&5], 1);
assert_eq!(counts.get(&0), None);

    use itertools::Itertools;
let counts = [1, 1, 1, 3, 3, 5].iter().counts();
assert_eq!(counts[&1], 3);
assert_eq!(counts[&3], 2);
assert_eq!(counts[&5], 1);
assert_eq!(counts.get(&0), None);
    Run
    Source

    counts_with_hasher

    fn counts_with_hasher(self: Self, hash_builder: S) -> HashMap<<Self as >::Item, usize, S>

    Collect the items in this iterator and return a HashMap the same way .counts() does, but use the specified hash builder for hashing.

    Source

    counts_by

    fn counts_by(self: Self, f: F) -> HashMap<K, usize>

    Collect the items in this iterator and return a HashMap which contains each item that appears in the iterator and the number of times it appears, determining identity using a keying function.

    # use itertools::Itertools;
struct Character {
    first_name: &'static str,
  # #<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">allow(dead_code)</code>
    last_name: &'static str,
}

let characters =
    vec![
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Pond"      },
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Wong"      },
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Santiago"  },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Bond"      },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Sullivan"  },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Norington" },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Kirk"      },
    ];

let first_name_frequency =
    characters
        .into_iter()
        .counts_by(|c| c.first_name);

assert_eq!(first_name_frequency<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&quot;Amy&quot;</code>, 3);
assert_eq!(first_name_frequency<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&quot;James&quot;</code>, 4);
assert_eq!(first_name_frequency.contains_key("Asha"), false);
    struct Character {
    first_name: &'static str,
    last_name: &'static str,
}

let characters =
    vec![
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Pond"      },
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Wong"      },
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Santiago"  },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Bond"      },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Sullivan"  },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Norington" },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Kirk"      },
    ];

let first_name_frequency =
    characters
        .into_iter()
        .counts_by(|c| c.first_name);

assert_eq!(first_name_frequency["Amy"], 3);
assert_eq!(first_name_frequency["James"], 4);
assert_eq!(first_name_frequency.contains_key("Asha"), false);

    use itertools::Itertools;
struct Character {
    first_name: &'static str,
  #[allow(dead_code)]
    last_name: &'static str,
}

let characters =
    vec![
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Pond"      },
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Wong"      },
        Character { first_name: "Amy",   last_name: "Santiago"  },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Bond"      },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Sullivan"  },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Norington" },
        Character { first_name: "James", last_name: "Kirk"      },
    ];

let first_name_frequency =
    characters
        .into_iter()
        .counts_by(|c| c.first_name);

assert_eq!(first_name_frequency["Amy"], 3);
assert_eq!(first_name_frequency["James"], 4);
assert_eq!(first_name_frequency.contains_key("Asha"), false);
    Run
    Source

    counts_by_with_hasher

    fn counts_by_with_hasher(self: Self, f: F, hash_builder: S) -> HashMap<K, usize, S>

    Collect the items in this iterator and return a HashMap the same way .counts_by() does, but use the specified hash builder for hashing.

    Source

    multiunzip

    fn multiunzip(self: Self) -> FromI

    Converts an iterator of tuples into a tuple of containers.

    It consumes an entire iterator of n-ary tuples, producing n collections, one for each column.

    This function is, in some sense, the opposite of multizip.

    use itertools::Itertools;

let inputs = vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">(1, 2, 3), (4, 5, 6), (7, 8, 9)</code>;

let (a, b, c): (Vec<_>, Vec<_>, Vec<_>) = inputs
    .into_iter()
    .multiunzip();

assert_eq!(a, vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 4, 7</code>);
assert_eq!(b, vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">2, 5, 8</code>);
assert_eq!(c, vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">3, 6, 9</code>);
    use itertools::Itertools;

let inputs = vec![(1, 2, 3), (4, 5, 6), (7, 8, 9)];

let (a, b, c): (Vec<_>, Vec<_>, Vec<_>) = inputs
    .into_iter()
    .multiunzip();

assert_eq!(a, vec![1, 4, 7]);
assert_eq!(b, vec![2, 5, 8]);
assert_eq!(c, vec![3, 6, 9]);
    Run
    Source

    try_len

    fn try_len(self: &Self) -> Result<usize, (usize, Option<usize>)>

    Returns the length of the iterator if one exists. Otherwise return self.size_hint().

    Fallible ExactSizeIterator::len.

    Inherits guarantees and restrictions from Iterator::size_hint.

    use itertools::Itertools;

assert_eq!(<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">0; 10</code>.iter().try_len(), Ok(10));
assert_eq!((10..15).try_len(), Ok(5));
assert_eq!((15..10).try_len(), Ok(0));
assert_eq!((10..).try_len(), Err((usize::MAX, None)));
assert_eq!((10..15).filter(|x| x % 2 == 0).try_len(), Err((0, Some(5))));
    use itertools::Itertools;

assert_eq!([0; 10].iter().try_len(), Ok(10));
assert_eq!((10..15).try_len(), Ok(5));
assert_eq!((15..10).try_len(), Ok(0));
assert_eq!((10..).try_len(), Err((usize::MAX, None)));
assert_eq!((10..15).filter(|x| x % 2 == 0).try_len(), Err((0, Some(5))));
    Run
    Source

    strip_prefix

    fn strip_prefix( self: Self, prefix: Prefix ) -> Result<Self, StripPrefixError<Self, <Prefix as >::IntoIter, <Self as >::Item>>

    Removes a prefix from the iterator, returning the rest.

    If self begins with all the items yielded by prefix (in order), this returns Ok of the iterator advanced past that prefix. Otherwise it returns Err(StripPrefixError { .. }) exposing the partially-consumed iterator, the remaining prefix, and the items that failed to match, so callers can recover progress made before the mismatch.

    See strip_prefix_by for a variant taking an explicit equality predicate.

    use itertools::Itertools;

let ok = (1..6).strip_prefix(<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 2</code>).map(Itertools::collect_vec).ok();
assert_eq!(ok, Some(vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">3, 4, 5</code>));
assert!((1..6).strip_prefix(<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 9</code>).is_err());
let empty = (1..6).strip_prefix(std::iter::empty::<i32>()).map(Itertools::collect_vec).ok();
assert_eq!(empty, Some(vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 2, 3, 4, 5</code>));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let ok = (1..6).strip_prefix([1, 2]).map(Itertools::collect_vec).ok();
assert_eq!(ok, Some(vec![3, 4, 5]));
assert!((1..6).strip_prefix([1, 9]).is_err());
let empty = (1..6).strip_prefix(std::iter::empty::<i32>()).map(Itertools::collect_vec).ok();
assert_eq!(empty, Some(vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5]));
    Run
    Source

    strip_prefix_by

    fn strip_prefix_by( self: Self, prefix: Prefix, eq: F ) -> Result<Self, StripPrefixError<Self, <Prefix as >::IntoIter, <Self as >::Item>>

    Removes a prefix from the iterator using eq to compare items.

    If self begins with all the items yielded by prefix (in order, as judged by eq), this returns Ok of the iterator advanced past that prefix. Otherwise it returns Err(StripPrefixError { .. }), allowing the prefix items to have a different type than Self::Item.

    use itertools::Itertools;

let path = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&quot;home&quot;, &quot;user&quot;, &quot;file&quot;</code>;
let stripped = path.iter().strip_prefix_by(<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&quot;home&quot;, &quot;user&quot;</code>, |a, b| **a == *b);
assert_eq!(stripped.map(Itertools::collect_vec).ok(), Some(vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">&amp;&quot;file&quot;</code>));
    use itertools::Itertools;

let path = ["home", "user", "file"];
let stripped = path.iter().strip_prefix_by(["home", "user"], |a, b| **a == *b);
assert_eq!(stripped.map(Itertools::collect_vec).ok(), Some(vec![&"file"]));
    Run

    Trait Implementations §

    impl Itertools for T

    Functions §

    Source

    equal §

    fn equal(a: I, b: J) -> bool

    Return true if both iterables produce equal sequences (elements pairwise equal and sequences of the same length), false otherwise.

    IntoIterator enabled version of Iterator::eq.

    assert!(itertools::equal(vec!<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">1, 2, 3</code>, 1..4));
assert!(!itertools::equal(&<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">0, 0</code>, &<code title="unresolved intra-doc link">0, 0, 0</code>));
    assert!(itertools::equal(vec![1, 2, 3], 1..4));
assert!(!itertools::equal(&[0, 0], &[0, 0, 0]));
    Run
    Source

    assert_equal §

    fn assert_equal(a: I, b: J)

    Assert that two iterables produce equal sequences, with the same semantics as equal(a, b).

    Panics on assertion failure with a message that shows the two different elements and the iteration index.

    # use itertools::assert_equal;
assert_equal("exceed".split('c'), "excess".split('c'));
// ^PANIC: panicked at 'Failed assertion Some("eed") == Some("ess") for iteration 1'.
    should_panic
    assert_equal("exceed".split('c'), "excess".split('c'));
// ^PANIC: panicked at 'Failed assertion Some("eed") == Some("ess") for iteration 1'.

    use itertools::assert_equal;
assert_equal("exceed".split('c'), "excess".split('c'));
// ^PANIC: panicked at 'Failed assertion Some("eed") == Some("ess") for iteration 1'.
    Run
    Source

    partition §

    fn partition(iter: I, pred: F) -> usize

    Partition a sequence using predicate pred so that elements that map to true are placed before elements which map to false.

    The order within the partitions is arbitrary.

    Return the index of the split point.

    use itertools::partition;

# // use repeated numbers to not promise any ordering
let mut data = <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">7, 1, 1, 7, 1, 1, 7</code>;
let split_index = partition(&mut data, |elt| *elt >= 3);

assert_eq!(data, <code title="unresolved intra-doc link">7, 7, 7, 1, 1, 1, 1</code>);
assert_eq!(split_index, 3);
    use itertools::partition;

let mut data = [7, 1, 1, 7, 1, 1, 7];
let split_index = partition(&mut data, |elt| *elt >= 3);

assert_eq!(data, [7, 7, 7, 1, 1, 1, 1]);
assert_eq!(split_index, 3);

    use itertools::partition;

// use repeated numbers to not promise any ordering
let mut data = [7, 1, 1, 7, 1, 1, 7];
let split_index = partition(&mut data, |elt| *elt >= 3);

assert_eq!(data, [7, 7, 7, 1, 1, 1, 1]);
assert_eq!(split_index, 3);
    Run
